topic: Network exposure aliasing by finite graph-pair certificate: compare the six-cycle C6 with two disjoint triangles under iid Bernoulli treatment.

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize finite graph-pair aliases, including C6 versus two disjoint triangles, whose Bernoulli one-hop treated-neighbor count laws agree for every unit and every p but whose local spillover estimands differ through second-order exposure geometry.

source_refs.claim: Exposure mappings are the design-to-estimand bridge in general-interference experiments, so the chosen exposure map is a load-bearing object rather than a harmless summary.
source_refs.author: Peter M. Aronow and Cyrus Samii

source_refs.claim: The paper argues that exposure mappings used to define effects should be separated from exposure mappings used as assumptions about the true interference structure.
source_refs.author: Fredrik Savje

source_refs.claim: Network misspecification can be treated through induced exposure probabilities and multi-network robustness, but equivalent exposure probabilities need not encode all graph geometry.
source_refs.author: Bar Weinstein and Daniel Nevo

source_refs.excerpt: Include a four-unit witness table for Conjecture 1 showing one concrete cellwise endpoint
Include a minimal four-unit or three-edge witness candidate for Conjecture 1
The exact minimal number of units needed for strict containment is intentionally left open.

open_problems.why_unsolved: The published literature makes exposure mappings central and then studies misspecification or network robustness at the level of exposure effects, induced exposure probabilities, or broad assumptions, while the prior CausalSmith network-exposure reviewers asked for a concrete finite witness rather than another verbal strict-containment claim. The remaining gap is the exact finite algebraic case where marginal one-hop exposure histograms match perfectly under iid Bernoulli treatment but the shared graph geometry, such as two-hop paths or triangle closure, changes a spillover estimand.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to standardize by the scalar one-hop exposure count fails because Savje separates exposure definitions from structural assumptions and the prior reviewers demand a witness preserving unitwise marginals while exposing shared-graph incompatibility.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A resolution would be an equivalence-class theorem or fully enumerated graph-pair table proving identical one-hop count laws and a separating closed-form spillover/ADE contrast for C6 versus 2K3.

open_problems.open_problem: Determine whether restricted exposure-map specification diagnostics have power on finite degree-count alias classes, or whether C6-versus-2K3-style graph-pair alternatives inherit the general impossibility of exposure-map testing.

source_refs.claim: The paper proposes randomization tests for spillover-specification hypotheses using hierarchical relations among exposure specifications.
source_refs.author: Tadao Hoshino and Takahide Yanagi

source_refs.claim: The paper proves that unrestricted tests of exposure-mapping models against larger exposure alternatives have worst-case Type I and Type II errors summing to one, so informative tests need restricted alternatives.
source_refs.author: Chao Gao, Christopher Harshaw, Fredrik Savje and Yitan Wang

source_refs.excerpt: Sharpen Conjecture 2 by naming the exact certificate family intended
Add zero-exposure incompatibility constraints to Conjecture 2's certificate formulation
replaced the cut display with a capacitated-incidence certificate statement

open_problems.why_unsolved: Published testing work is pulled in two directions: Hoshino and Yanagi construct broadly applicable randomization tests, while Gao, Harshaw, Savje, and Wang show that unrestricted exposure-model specification testing is impossible without narrowing the alternative. The prior proposal's certificate route was also underspecified, with reviewers asking for the exact infeasibility or graph-feasibility system, so the finite alias class has not yet been pinned down as either testable or provably indistinguishable.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to run a generic exposure-mapping specification test fails because Gao-Harshaw-Savje-Wang show worst-case Type I and Type II errors sum to one unless the alternative model is further restricted.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A resolution would be a finite-sample test, impossibility theorem, or necessary-and-sufficient certificate system for the restricted graph-pair alternative indexed by a triangle-closure or exposure-covariance contrast.

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize the minimal rooted local-neighborhood information needed to distinguish graphs that are aliased by marginal treated-neighbor count exposures under iid Bernoulli assignment.

source_refs.claim: The local approach represents exposure through rooted local network configurations and pools outcomes across similarly configured agents, not through scalar count exposures alone.
source_refs.author: Eric Auerbach, Hongchang Guo and Max Tabord-Meehan

source_refs.claim: The Biometrika discussion argues that misspecified exposure effects are not automatically useful for policy unless the exposure captures substantively relevant interference structure.
source_refs.author: Eric Auerbach, Jonathan Auerbach and Max Tabord-Meehan

open_problems.why_unsolved: Rooted-network approaches point beyond scalar one-hop summaries, and the policy-usefulness discussion warns that misspecified exposure effects can lose substantive meaning. What is still missing for the graph-pair setting is a sharp finite boundary: which local motif radius or rooted configuration statistic is necessary and sufficient to separate count-aliased graphs such as C6 and 2K3 for a spillover estimand.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to pool all units with the same treated-neighbor count fails because Auerbach-Guo-Tabord-Meehan define similarity through rooted local configurations rather than scalar neighbor-treatment counts.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A resolution would be a minimal-sufficiency theorem or counterexample identifying the smallest rooted local configuration class that separates Bernoulli count-aliased graphs.

open_problems.open_problem: Give a finite two-hop or triangle-closure spillover contrast showing when one-hop exposure truncation is marginally aliased but biased for an approximate-neighborhood-interference estimand.

source_refs.claim: Approximate neighborhood interference weakens exact neighborhood truncation by allowing farther alters to have smaller but nonzero effects.
source_refs.author: Michael P. Leung

source_refs.claim: The paper studies average treatment effects under limited but otherwise unknown interference and notes that conventional confidence statements may fail under interference.
source_refs.author: Fredrik Savje, Peter M. Aronow and Michael G. Hudgens

open_problems.why_unsolved: Leung shows that exact one-hop truncation can be violated even when more distant interference decays, and Savje-Aronow-Hudgens show that useful average effects can exist under limited unknown interference. Neither source provides a small finite witness in which the one-hop exposure distribution is exactly the same across two graphs but the two-hop or triangle-closure geometry changes the estimand, which is exactly the missing bridge to the C6 versus 2K3 certificate.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to truncate the exposure mapping at treated first neighbors fails because Leung's approximate-neighborhood-interference model allows alters beyond the ego neighborhood to have smaller but nonzero effects.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A resolution would be a finite counterexample with a closed-form two-hop or triangle-closure spillover contrast and a regime statement identifying the bias from one-hop truncation.

literature_map:
Hudgens and Halloran (2008, JASA, doi:10.1198/016214508000000292) define direct, indirect, total, and overall effects under interference and anchor the modern estimand vocabulary.
Aronow and Samii (2017, Annals of Applied Statistics, doi:10.1214/16-AOAS1005) make exposure mappings central: design, mapping, and estimand jointly define the analysis.
Forastiere, Airoldi and Mealli (2021, JASA, doi:10.1080/01621459.2020.1768100) identify treatment and interference effects in observational networks using generalized propensity scores and neighborhood exposure.
Savje, Aronow and Hudgens (2021, Annals of Statistics, doi:10.1214/20-AOS1973) show standard estimators can target an expected ATE under limited unknown interference, but conventional confidence statements may fail.
Leung (2020, Review of Economics and Statistics, doi:10.1162/rest_a_00818) derives estimation and inference for treatment and spillover effects under network dependence.
Leung (2022, Econometrica, doi:10.3982/ECTA17841) weakens exact neighborhood interference through approximate neighborhood interference, allowing decaying effects beyond one hop.
Savje (2024, Biometrika, doi:10.1093/biomet/asad019) separates exposure effects used as definitions from exposure mappings used as structural assumptions.
Auerbach, Guo and Tabord-Meehan (2026 QE / arXiv:2105.03810) replace scalar exposure summaries with rooted local network configurations and a network-sieve local approach.
Auerbach, Auerbach and Tabord-Meehan (2024, Biometrika discussion, doi:10.1093/biomet/asad054) argue misspecified exposure effects are not automatically policy-useful.
Hoshino and Yanagi (2023, arXiv:2301.05580) propose randomization tests for interference-structure specifications using exposure hierarchies.
Gao, Harshaw, Savje and Wang (2026, arXiv:2605.09726) prove an impossibility theorem for unrestricted exposure-mapping specification tests, pushing attention to restricted alternatives.
Li, Sussman and Kolaczyk (2021, arXiv:2105.04518) study bias and variance from noisy network measurements under Bernoulli treatment and discrete exposure models.
Weinstein and Nevo (2026, Biometrics, doi:10.1093/biomtc/ujag023) study misspecified network interference structures, induced exposure probabilities, robust multi-network estimators, and non-unique correct networks.
Schroder, Oprescu, Feuerriegel and Kallus (2026, arXiv:2602.03459) provide sharp partial-identification bounds for misspecified network exposure mappings, but not a finite marginal-histogram alias certificate.
prior_proposal_map:
Mined adjacent banked proposal pid_network_exposure_v1 under _bank/candidates, including its final proposal, README, and reviewer JSONs angle0_v1 through angle0_v4.
The highest-yield Track-A signals for the current graph-pair aliasing topic are pid_network_exposure_v1 reviewer requests for a four-unit or three-edge finite witness, a Theta_cell projection, and exact graph-feasibility certificates.
The pid_network_exposure_v1 honest-scope flags leave strict nonsharpness, cut/certificate statements, and the exact minimal number of units open.
Mined failed upgrade line pid_network_iv_exposure_v1/v2/v3 under _bank/failed; v1/v2 contain little mathematical evidence, while v3 contains proposals, rejected angles, gaps, and reviewer JSONs.
pid_network_iv_exposure_v3 reviewers repeatedly requested complete finite experiments with assignment support, take-up tables, outcome tables, support functionals, and shared-completion versus stitched/product comparisons.
pid_network_iv_exposure_v3 rejected angle3 is relevant as a finite pair/equality-check template, but its IV-specific gaps were filtered out because the current topic is a non-IV graph-pair exposure aliasing certificate.
